source.title: PreMode predicts mode-of-action of missense variants by deep graph representation learning of protein sequence and structural context

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Predicting mode-of-action (molecular and genetic) of missense variants is challenging because existing methods focus on binary pathogenicity, which does not capture the multi-dimensional functional impacts. Mode-of-action is gene-specific, with limited labeled data per gene, and requires understanding of protein sequence and structural context. The paper proposes PreMode, a deep graph representation learning method using SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks, pretrained on pathogenicity and fine-tuned via transfer learning to predict mode-of-action.

section_gists.heading: PreMode predicts mode-of-action of missense variants by deep graph representation learning of protein sequence and structural context
section_gists.gist: Abstract and introduction: Missense variants have multi-dimensional functional impacts beyond binary pathogenicity. Existing methods predict pathogenicity but not mode-of-action (e.g., gain/loss-of-function). PreMode uses SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks on protein sequences and structures to predict gene-specific mode-of-action. It achieves state-of-the-art performance via transfer learning, validated on molecular and genetic levels, and enables efficient DMS study design and active learning for protein engineering.
process_signals: Problem: Pathogenicity prediction is insufficient; mode-of-action is needed for clinical and protein engineering applications.
Hypothesis: Graph representation learning with SE(3)-equivariance can capture mode-of-action by modeling protein context.
Approach: Pretrain on pathogenicity, then transfer learn on mode-of-action tasks.
methods: SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks
Transfer learning from pathogenicity to mode-of-action

section_gists.heading: Overview
section_gists.gist: Framework overview: Mode-of-action is defined at molecular (biochemical property changes) and genetic (GoF/LoF) levels. The predictor uses two parameters: distance from wild type (r) for pathogenicity and direction of change (θ) for mode-of-action. r is learned from all genes (pretraining), θ is learned per gene/protein family via transfer learning.
process_signals: Conceptual framework: r and θ parameters separate pathogenicity from mode-of-action.
Design choice: Pretrain on pathogenicity (shared across genes), then transfer learn θ (gene-specific).
methods: Two-parameter model (r, θ)
Pretraining on pathogenicity, transfer learning for mode-of-action

section_gists.heading: Curation and characterization of mode-of-action labeled missense variants
section_gists.gist: Curated largest-to-date mode-of-action datasets: 41,081 DMS variants in 8 genes (molecular), 2043 GoF and 7889 LoF variants in ~1300 genes (genetic). Characterization: GoF variants are more in low pLDDT regions and surfaces, LoF variants have bigger folding energy impact. Both are in conserved regions. GoF/LoF distribution is uneven across genes, necessitating gene-specific models.
process_signals: Data constraint: Limited labeled GoF/LoF variants per gene, uneven distribution.
Observation: Structural and evolutionary features differ between GoF and LoF, but patterns vary by protein family.
Implication: Need for protein-specific predictive models.
methods: Data curation from literature, databases, cancer hotspots
Characterization using AlphaFold2 pLDDT, RSA, folding energy, conservation

section_gists.heading: A deep learning model for mode-of-action predictions
section_gists.gist: PreMode model: Inputs include amino acid properties, ESM2 embeddings, AlphaFold2 structures, MSA. Uses SE(3)-equivariant graph attention: star-graph (variant to all residues) then KNN-graph (second-order effects). Pretrained on 148,324 pathogenic/benign variants. Ablation: ESM2, MSA, structure all contribute; removing paralogs drops AUROC most. Pretraining achieves AUROC 0.932 on independent test set.
process_signals: Design: SE(3)-equivariant graph attention to capture geometric context.
Pretraining objective: Pathogenicity prediction as sanity check.
Ablation insights: ESM2 embeddings, MSA, and structure are important; paralog removal causes largest drop, indicating potential overfitting to gene families.
methods: SE(3)-equivariant graph neural network with star and KNN graphs
Input features: ESM2 embeddings, MSA, secondary structure, pLDDT, sidechain coordinates, co-evolution, contact predictions
Pretraining on ClinVar, HGMD, PrimateAI data
failures_or_limits: Performance drops when removing paralogous genes from pretraining (AUROC 0.873), suggesting reliance on gene family information.

section_gists.heading: PreMode reaches state-of-the-art in molecular mode-of-action predictions and facilitates interpretation of deep mutational scan experiments
section_gists.gist: Molecular mode-of-action: Tested on DMS data for 8 genes (stability, activity, binding). PreMode outperforms baselines (Augmented ESM1b, EVE, etc.) with Spearman correlation ~0.6. Efficient transfer learning: needs ~40% of variants (~2000) to saturate performance. Applications: detecting noisy DMS measurements, cross-gene stability prediction.
process_signals: Success: PreMode outperforms all baselines on molecular tasks.
Efficiency: Transfer learning saturates at ~40% of DMS data.
Utility: Can identify abnormal experimental measurements and generalize stability predictions across genes.
methods: Transfer learning on DMS data (80/20 split, 5-fold)
Comparison with Augmented ESM1b, EVmutation, Unirep, EVE, ESM2+SLP
metrics: Better than baselines at ≥20% training data

section_gists.heading: PreMode is state-of-the-art in genetic mode-of-action predictions
section_gists.gist: Genetic mode-of-action (GoF/LoF): Per-gene transfer learning on 9 genes with ≥15 GoF/LoF variants. PreMode achieves AUC 0.7-0.9, outperforming random forest, non-pretrained model, and cross-gene model. Better than LoGoFunc and funNCion. Ablation: ESM, structure, paralog removal all important. Few-shot learning: PreMode outperforms random forest even with 10% data. Domain-based transfer improves performance.
process_signals: Key finding: Per-gene transfer learning is necessary; cross-gene models fail due to gene-specific mechanisms and data imbalance.
Success: PreMode is state-of-the-art, robust to limited data.
Ablation: Structure input less important for FGFR2 (low pLDDT region), PTM info helps in some genes.
Domain-based transfer: Using data from same domain across genes improves predictions.
methods: Per-gene transfer learning with 4-fold CV ensemble
Comparison with random forest, LoGoFunc, funNCion
Ablation: removing ESM, structure, paralogs
Few-shot: subsampling training data
Domain-based transfer learning
metrics: Better than baselines in few-shot settings
failures_or_limits: FGFR2 performance low (AUC <0.6) for all models; possibly due to low pLDDT region.
Cross-gene model performs poorly, sometimes random or reversed predictions.

section_gists.heading: PreMode predicted mode-of-action landscapes in individual proteins through in silico saturated mutagenesis
section_gists.gist: In silico saturated mutagenesis on BRAF, TP53, etc. PreMode identifies GoF-enriched regions. In BRAF, GoF variants in zinc-finger domain destabilize autoinhibitory 14-3-3 binding but maintain Ras binding; kinase domain GoF (V600E) destabilizes inactive state, stabilizes active state. TP53 GoF regions include post-translational modification sites. Validates biological plausibility.
process_signals: Validation: Predictions align with known structural mechanisms (e.g., BRAF V600E).
Interpretation: FoldX energy calculations support PreMode's GoF/LoF assignments.
methods: In silico saturated mutagenesis
FoldX energy calculations on active/inactive conformations

section_gists.heading: PreMode trained with active learning allows efficient few-shot transfer
section_gists.gist: Active learning for protein design: On GFP fluorescence, PreMode with active learning achieves Spearman correlation 0.69 using only 40% of data, outperforming random subsampling.
process_signals: Extension: Active learning improves data efficiency for protein engineering.
methods: Active learning: iteratively select variants with highest predicted variance

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Summary: PreMode addresses mode-of-action prediction via pretraining and transfer learning. Limitations: binary G/LoF prediction misses complexity (e.g., ion channel conformations, Muller's morphs). Static structure may not capture dynamics, especially in low pLDDT regions. Future: incorporate protein dynamics, more labeled data for finer categories.
process_signals: Limitation: Binary G/LoF is oversimplified; need for more nuanced categories.
Limitation: Reliance on static AlphaFold2 structures; low performance in disordered regions.
Future work: Protein dynamics, larger labeled datasets.
failures_or_limits: Binary G/LoF prediction does not capture full complexity.
Lower performance in low pLDDT regions due to static structure representation.

section_gists.heading: Training and testing datasets
section_gists.gist: Datasets: GoF/LoF from multiple sources (2043 GoF, 7889 LoF). Per-gene split: 80/20, 5-fold, only genes with ≥15 GoF and ≥12 LoF used. Pathogenicity: 148,324 variants (ClinVar, HGMD, PrimateAI). Independent test: 533 cancer hotspots vs 533 benign. DMS: 8 genes from MAVEDB.
process_signals: Data curation: Largest-to-date mode-of-action dataset.
Evaluation design: Per-gene splits to avoid data leakage; tailored splits for fair comparison with LoGoFunc and funNCion.
methods: Data curation from multiple sources
Per-gene train/test split with minimum variant thresholds

section_gists.heading: Model architectures
section_gists.gist: PreMode architecture: 4-layer SE(3)-equivariant graph attention. Layer 1: feature embedding (node invariant, node structure, edge invariant). Layers 2-3: equivariant graph attention on star graph (first-order effects) then KNN graph (second-order). Layer 4: graph attention summary for prediction. Maintains SE(3)-equivariance by separate invariant and structure feature updates.
process_signals: Design choice: Star graph for direct variant-residue interactions, KNN for indirect effects.
Equivariance: No bias in structure feature embedding; attention mechanism shares information between invariant and structure features.
methods: SE(3)-equivariant graph attention
Star graph and KNN graph
Feature embedding: ESM2, MSA, structure, etc.

section_gists.heading: Model training and testing
section_gists.gist: Training: Adam optimizer. Pretraining: lr 1e-4, batch 256, 20 epochs, 5% validation, warmup+decay. Transfer learning (molecular): batch 8, 40 epochs. Transfer learning (genetic): 20 epochs, 4-fold CV ensemble, window sizes 251/1251. Hardware: 4 GPUs for pretraining (50h), 1 GPU for transfer (4-6h molecular, 20min-1h genetic).
process_signals: Training strategy: Warmup and learning rate decay on plateau.
Genetic task: Ensemble of 4-fold CV models with different window sizes to improve robustness.
Computational cost: Pretraining expensive, transfer learning fast.
methods: Adam optimizer
Learning rate schedule with warmup and decay
4-fold cross-validation ensemble for genetic tasks

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper hypothesizes that mode-of-action can be predicted by separating pathogenicity (distance from wild type) from direction of change, and that SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks can capture the necessary protein context.

The research trajectory moves from problem identification (limitations of pathogenicity prediction) to data curation (largest mode-of-action dataset), model design (PreMode with pretraining and transfer learning), validation on molecular and genetic tasks, and finally extensions to in silico mutagenesis and active learning.

Key revisions include the use of per-gene transfer learning instead of a cross-gene model due to poor generalizability, and the incorporation of domain-based transfer to leverage data from related proteins.
Limitations acknowledged: binary G/LoF oversimplification, static structure limitations, and need for protein dynamics.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How well does PreMode generalize to genes with very few (<15) labeled variants?
Can the model be extended to predict more nuanced mode-of-action categories (e.g., Muller's morphs)?
What is the impact of incorporating protein dynamics or alternative conformations on prediction accuracy, especially in disordered regions?
How does the active learning strategy perform on other protein engineering tasks beyond GFP?

events.summary: Pathogenicity prediction does not capture multi-dimensional functional impacts; mode-of-action is needed for clinical and protein engineering applications.
Mode-of-action prediction should be gene-specific due to diverse protein functions; a universal predictor leads to conceptual ambiguity.
Characterization: GoF variants more in low pLDDT regions and surfaces; LoF variants have bigger folding energy impact; both in conserved regions.
Limited availability of mode-of-action data for most genes and protein families is a main challenge.

events.summary: PreMode uses SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks with star-graph and KNN-graph to capture direct and second-order impacts of amino acid alterations.
PreMode outperformed all methods in molecular mode-of-action prediction with Spearman correlation ~0.6, and transfer learning saturates at ~40% of DMS data.
PreMode can identify abnormal DMS measurements by comparing predictions to experimental readouts, and stability models generalize across genes.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers model design, pretraining results, molecular and genetic mode-of-action experiments, and limitations.

events.summary: PreMode compared against baseline methods on genetic mode-of-action prediction for 9 genes, achieving average AUC 0.8-0.9 in some genes and 0.7-0.8 in others.
PreMode outperformed LoGoFunc in all genes except CACNA1A, where performance was similar.
PreMode outperformed funNCion in ion channel genes SCN2A, SCN5A, CACNA1A.
Ablation analysis showed removing ESM, homologous proteins in pretrain, or protein structure decreased PreMode's overall performance.
PreMode outperformed random forest in few-shot transfer learning, even with only 10% of training data.
Domain-based transfer learning using data from same domain across genes improved performance over gene-only training.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains key experimental results and analyses for genetic mode-of-action prediction, including comparisons with baselines, ablation studies, few-shot learning, and domain-based transfer learning.

events.summary: PreMode identified variants that only disrupt stability but not enzyme function, and vice versa, in PTEN.
PreMode predicts binary G/LoF, which does not capture full complexity of protein functional changes.
PreMode has lower performance for genes where G/LoF variants are in low pLDDT regions, due to static structure representation.
PreMode's architecture includes a feature embedding layer, two equivariant graph attention layers, and a final graph attention layer for prediction.
Input features include ESM2 embeddings, DSSP secondary structure, pLDDT, MSA frequencies, and side chain coordinates.
Edge features include weighted MSA covariance, Euclidean distances, and contact predictions from MSA transformer.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers analysis of PTEN variants, active learning on GFP, discussion of limitations, and detailed model architecture and input features.

events.summary: PreMode uses SE(3)-equivariant graph attention layers with star and KNN graphs to capture first- and second-order effects.
Edge features encode co-evolution, distance, and contact strength.
Pretraining on pathogenicity dataset took about 50 hours on 4 NVIDIA A40 GPUs.
Transfer learning on molecular tasks took 4-6 hours on a single GPU.
Transfer learning on genetic tasks used 4-fold cross-validation ensemble and took 20 min to 1 hour.
Active learning experiments iteratively selected variants with highest model uncertainty to improve data efficiency.

evidence_bank.quote: pathogenicity does not capture the complexity of functional and genetic effects of variants
Such limitation reduced the utility of the methods in genetic analysis and clinical applications.

mode-of-action centers around how a variant disrupts the normal function of a protein. Given the inherent diversity of protein functions, attempting to define a universally applicable predictive task for all G/LoF variants across all proteins could lead to conceptual ambiguity.
we propose that such predictive tasks should be defined within the context of individual proteins or protein families that share similar functions.
GoF variants are relatively more likely to be located in regions with lower pLDDT (50 < pLDDT < 90) than LoF variants, and are relatively more likely to be on protein surfaces (rsa ≥ 25%)
LoF variants have an overall bigger impact on protein folding energy than GoF variants
both LoF and GoF variants are more likely to be in conserved regions than non-conserved regions
The main challenge is the limited availability of data for most genes and protein families.

PreMode applies SE(3)-equivariant mechanisms on edge features and node features first through a star-graph that connects variant site with all other residues to capture the direct impacts of amino acid alterations on other residues, then through the k-nearest neighbor (KNN)-graph that connects each residue with its closest neighbor residues to capture the second order impacts
PreMode outperformed all methods with higher Spearman correlation (Fig. 4a) on all the assays of 8 genes.

PreMode is able to accurately predict variant effects of all missense variants with around 40% (-2000) of variants measured inside one gene, after which increasing the number of data points will have minimal improvement
We showed that this difference value is highly correlated to the difference between the readout of the single biological replicate and average readouts in all experiments

A general model across all genes will have a poor generalizability and will perform random or nearly reversed predictions for unseen genes

the number of G/LoF variants are extremely unbalanced across genes. A deep neural network model with transfer-learning across genes will potentially reach a local minimum where gene-properties dominate its predictions

PreMode has similar performance as LoGoFunc in CACNA1A but better in all other genes
PreMode is better than funNCion in all three genes SCN2A, SCN5A, CACNA1A
Removing ESM, homologous proteins in pretrain or protein structure information decreased PreMode's overall performances in 9 genes
PreMode's overall performances in 9 genes were better than random forest even if we down sample the training data to 10% of whole dataset
We observed increased performance in all of the domains when using data across genes rather than using the gene alone
PreMode identified variants that only disrupt stability but not enzyme function
PreMode identified variants that only disrupt enzyme function but not stability, all located in the phosphatase domain
PreMode currently predicts a binary label as G/LoF. This is a limitation as it does not capture the complexity of protein functional changes.

PreMode can be potentially improved by considering protein dynamics, as ablation experiments showed it has lower performance for genes where both G/LoF variants are located in regions with relatively low pLDDT values.
Currently, PreMode includes features representing a static protein structure, which is not sufficient to model the variant effects in those regions.
PreMode is an SE(3)-equivariant graph attention neural network with 4 layers.
First layer is a feature embedding layer that encodes the input features into latent dimensions.
Second and third layers are equivariant graph attention layers designed to model interactions between the connected nodes
The last layer is a graph attention layer designed to summarize the overall impact of the variant on the protein and perform pathogenicity or mode-of-action prediction.

We collected a total of 148,324 missense variants for training, including 51,494 benign variants from PrimateAI $^{18}$ and 32,350 non-overlapping benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ with at least one-star non-conflict submits that labeled as “benign” or “likely-benign”; 64,480 “pathogenic” and “likely-pathogenic” variants from ClinVar database with at least one-star non-conflict submits $^{8}$ and non-overlapping variants from HGMD $^{41}$ (Table 2).

We collected 83,844 benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and PrimateAI $^{18}$ in 13,748 genes, and 64,480 pathogenic variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and HGMD $^{41}$ in 3703 genes (“Methods: Training and testing datasets”).
We split the DMS experiment datasets to \~40% of training data, \~10% of validation data and \~50% of testing data the same way as previous methods $^{79-81}$ .
We annotated 199 variants in hotspots of 27 oncogenic genes as GoF and 1506 variants in 248 tumor suppressor genes as LoF based on COSMIC database $^{51}$ .

The gain and loss of function-labeled variants were collected from literature searches $^{37,45,48,49}$ , cancer hotspots $^{50-52}$ , and published databases $^{53}$ (“Methods: Training and testing datasets”).

Instead, we used independent testing data for which the pathogenicity label was entirely based on statistical evidence, that is, 533 pathogenic missense variants in cancer hotspots from cBioportal $^{50,52,60}$ , and same number of benign variants in the same genes randomly selected from common variants in primates $^{18}$ .

We curated the largest-to-date mode-of-action labeled missense variants datasets annotated at both molecular and genetic levels, including 41,081 missense variants in eight genes with multidimensional measurements of different biochemical properties by DMS experiments curated from MAVEDB $^{6}$ , 2043 gain- and 7889 loss-of-function missense variants in \~1300 genes.

Gain and loss of function variants usually result in markedly different clinical phenotypes $^{37,42-45}$ , necessitating entirely distinct therapeutic approaches $^{30,36,37,46}$ .

For testing, we included 1066 missense variants, including 533 pathogenic missense variants from somatic missense hotspots in 153 cancer driver genes that not annotated in the training data; and 533 benign variants from the same genes, randomly selected from ClinVar and PrimateAI that not overlapping with training dataset (Table 2).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Predicting mode-of-action (molecular and genetic) of missense variants is challenging because existing methods focus on binary pathogenicity, which does not capture the multi-dimensional functional impacts. Mode-of-action is gene-specific, with limited labeled data per gene, and requires understanding of protein sequence and structural context.
